content.text: A headless browser runs the full browser engine — JavaScript execution, CSS layout, network requests, and rendering — without ever opening a visible window. It produces identical output to a headed browser: computed DOM state, screenshots, and PDFs. Removing the display layer makes it fast enough to run at scale in server environments and CI pipelines where no screen is available.
A headless browser is a browser process launched with display rendering disabled. In Chromium this is the

. The engine still runs the complete pipeline — HTML parsing, style computation, JavaScript VM, and an offscreen compositor — but composited frames are never presented to a display server. Instead, they are captured on demand as PNG or JPEG buffers.

Chromium introduced native headless mode in version 59 (2017) with what became known as Headless Shell. Chrome 112 shipped the new unified Headless mode (formerly called "new headless") that shares all code paths with the headed browser, closing gaps around extensions, background pages, and storage APIs that the old shell mode handled differently. Playwright and Puppeteer both target this new unified mode.

Server environments, containers, and CI agents do not have a physical or virtual display. Running a traditional browser requires an X11 server or Xvfb (a virtual framebuffer) to host the window, adding system complexity and memory overhead. Headless mode eliminates that dependency entirely, allowing a browser to run as an ordinary server process.

For AI agents, headless browsers are the standard deployment form. A cloud-hosted agent that browses the web on a user's behalf has no screen to display to; it only needs the DOM state and screenshots for the vision model. Headless operation reduces latency — compositing frames for display is skipped — and allows hundreds of browser instances to run in parallel on a headless server cluster.
No display required: runs on servers, Docker containers, and CI runners without X11 or Xvfb.
Lower resource consumption: skipping GPU compositing and on-screen rendering reduces CPU and memory footprint compared to headed mode.
Full JavaScript fidelity: the same V8 engine and Blink renderer as the headed browser; output is identical for any JS-heavy SPA.
Screenshot on demand: capture any page state as a PNG, JPEG, or WebP image without any visual flicker or window management.
PDF generation: print any web page to PDF with pixel-accurate CSS layout, fonts, and media queries — useful for report generation workflows.
Scale-friendly: no window manager limits concurrency; the bottleneck is CPU and memory rather than display slots.
Some websites detect headless browsers through JavaScript fingerprinting. The

in automated sessions, and certain browser properties differ from typical user browsers (e.g., zero plugins, no Chrome extensions, specific GPU renderer strings). Bot-detection services use these signals to identify and block headless traffic.

WebGL and GPU-accelerated rendering may behave differently in headless mode, particularly on servers without GPU hardware. Applications that rely on canvas or WebGL for rendering may produce different visual output, and performance benchmarks that exercise the GPU path will not be representative.
Debugging is harder: cannot visually watch the browser execute. Use --headed mode for development and headless for CI.
Browser fingerprinting leaks automation signals via navigator.webdriver, missing plugins, and unusual screen dimensions.
Audio and video playback may be limited or require additional flags, making media-heavy pages difficult to test accurately.
Memory still accumulates per open page; large-scale deployments require explicit context and page lifecycle management.

Chromium's rendering pipeline has a clear separation between the browser process (which manages tabs, extensions, and the address bar) and renderer processes (one per tab by default, which contain Blink and V8). In headed mode, the compositor thread transfers GPU texture tiles to the display. In headless mode the
flag replaces the display compositor with an offscreen compositor that writes frames to shared memory or returns them synchronously on screenshot requests via CDP.

The automation library opens a CDP WebSocket connection on the debug port (default 9222). Every action — navigate, click, screenshot — serializes to a JSON-RPC command on that socket. Chromium processes the command in the browser process, routes it to the correct renderer, and returns a result or error. Asynchronous events (page load, network request) are pushed as CDP events on the same socket.

over CDP. Chromium triggers a synchronous frame capture from the offscreen compositor, encodes it as base64 PNG or JPEG, and returns the binary data in the CDP response. The entire round-trip is typically under 100 ms for a standard viewport.

attrs.alt: Headless browser architecture — from automation code through CDP to the headless Chromium process and output
attrs.title: Headless Browser Architecture

content.text: Automation code talks to the headless Chromium process over a CDP WebSocket channel. Inside Chromium, the V8 engine runs JavaScript, Blink handles DOM and layout, and the network stack fetches resources. The offscreen compositor renders frames without a display. Screenshots and PDFs flow back to the automation code; DOM data is returned as structured CDP responses.

Use headless mode for all server-side and CI deployments. Switch to headed mode only during development when actively debugging an interaction: watching the browser act in real time reveals timing issues, unexpected redirects, and layout problems faster than reading logs.
CI/CD pipelines: run end-to-end tests on every pull request without a display server.
Cloud-hosted AI agents: browse the web on behalf of users without a real desktop environment.
Screenshot services: generate social preview images, open-graph thumbnails, or PDF reports from HTML templates.
Pre-rendering SPAs for SEO: server-side render JS-heavy pages to static HTML for search engine indexing.
Data extraction at scale: run hundreds of browser instances on worker machines without display overhead.
In Playwright, headless mode is the default when
. To switch between headed and headless, change a single launch argument. Additional Chromium flags control viewport size, device emulation, and GPU behavior. For bot-evasion,
patches several fingerprinting properties before page load.

import asyncio
from playwright.async_api import async_playwright

async def headless_demo():
    async with async_playwright() as pw:
        # headless=True is the default; shown explicitly for clarity
        browser = await pw.chromium.launch(
            headless=True,
            args=[
                "--no-sandbox",          # required in Docker / root containers
                "--disable-dev-shm-usage",  # prevents /dev/shm exhaustion in Docker
                "--disable-gpu",         # skip GPU init on servers without GPU
                "--disable-extensions",
            ]
        )
        context = await browser.new_context(
            viewport={"width": 1280, "height": 800},
            device_scale_factor=2,       # retina-quality screenshots
        )
        page = await context.new_page()
        await page.goto("https://playwright.dev", wait_until="networkidle")

        # Screenshot to bytes
        png_bytes = await page.screenshot(full_page=True, type="png")
        with open("playwright_dev.png", "wb") as f:
            f.write(png_bytes)

        # PDF generation
        pdf_bytes = await page.pdf(format="A4", print_background=True)
        with open("playwright_dev.pdf", "wb") as f:
            f.write(pdf_bytes)

        await browser.close()
        print(f"Screenshot: {len(png_bytes):,} bytes")
        print(f"PDF: {len(pdf_bytes):,} bytes")

asyncio.run(headless_demo())
Detecting headless mode from a page

import asyncio
from playwright.async_api import async_playwright

async def check_fingerprint():
    """Inspect how the browser appears to JavaScript running in the page."""
    async with async_playwright() as pw:
        browser = await pw.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        page = await browser.new_page()
        await page.goto("about:blank")

        # These properties are read by bot-detection scripts
        result = await page.evaluate("""
        () => ({
            webdriver:       navigator.webdriver,
            plugins:         navigator.plugins.length,
            languages:       navigator.languages,
            hardwareConcurrency: navigator.hardwareConcurrency,
            platform:        navigator.platform,
            userAgent:       navigator.userAgent,
        })
        """)
        await browser.close()
        return result

fingerprint = asyncio.run(check_fingerprint())
for k, v in fingerprint.items():
    print(f"  {k}: {v}")

import asyncio
from pathlib import Path
from playwright.async_api import async_playwright, Browser

async def screenshot_url(browser: Browser, url: str, out_dir: Path) -> str:
    """Capture a full-page screenshot and return the saved path."""
    context = await browser.new_context(
        viewport={"width": 1280, "height": 800}
    )
    page = await context.new_page()
    try:
        await page.goto(url, wait_until="networkidle", timeout=30_000)
        name = url.replace("https://", "").replace("/", "_")[:60]
        path = out_dir / f"{name}.png"
        await page.screenshot(path=str(path), full_page=True)
        return str(path)
    finally:
        await context.close()

async def batch_screenshots(urls: list[str], out_dir: str = "screenshots"):
    out = Path(out_dir)
    out.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
    async with async_playwright() as pw:
        # Share ONE browser process; each URL gets its own context
        browser = await pw.chromium.launch(
            headless=True,
            args=["--no-sandbox", "--disable-dev-shm-usage"]
        )
        tasks = [screenshot_url(browser, url, out) for url in urls]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
        await browser.close()
    return results

urls = [
    "https://playwright.dev",
    "https://docs.python.org",
    "https://github.com",
]
results = asyncio.run(batch_screenshots(urls))
for r in results:
    print(r)

A SaaS reporting platform generates weekly PDF dashboards for customers. Each dashboard is an HTML page rendered by a React application, containing charts, tables, and commentary. The generation pipeline uses a headless Chromium pool managed by Playwright. The platform runs 12 Chromium processes on a 16-core server. Each process handles one customer dashboard sequentially — load the React app, inject the customer's data into the page via
, wait for all charts to render (detected by a custom
with A4 format and print-background enabled. The PDF is uploaded to S3 and a pre-signed URL is emailed. Average generation time is 2.4 seconds per report.

Omitting --no-sandbox in Docker containers running as root. Chromium's sandbox uses setuid binaries that fail in rootless containers; --no-sandbox is required but should only be used in isolated container environments.
Using a tiny viewport for screenshots. The default 800x600 viewport clips modern responsive layouts; set a realistic width (1280px or wider) for accurate captures.

Capturing a screenshot before the page is visually ready. wait_until='load' fires when DOM parsing is done but before lazy-loaded images or deferred animations complete. Use wait_until='networkidle' or a custom signal for production screenshot services.
Spawning a new browser process per request. Browser startup takes 1-3 seconds. Maintain a warm browser pool and reuse instances, creating a new context per request.
Leaking contexts after errors. Always close contexts in a finally block or use async context managers; leaked contexts hold sockets and renderer processes open indefinitely.
Use a fixed Playwright version pinned in requirements.txt or package-lock.json to ensure consistent browser behavior across environments.
Set explicit timeouts on all page operations. Headless pages can hang indefinitely on slow or broken pages; default_navigation_timeout of 30 s prevents queue starvation.
Use --disable-dev-shm-usage and mount a larger /dev/shm in Docker to prevent shared-memory exhaustion on complex pages.
Intercept and block analytics and ad scripts with page.route() to reduce page load time and eliminate external tracking in automation sessions.
Switch to --headed mode during development by setting headless=False and adding slow_mo=100 (milliseconds between actions) to watch the browser in slow motion.

Headless Chromium consumes approximately 100–200 MB of RSS per browser context at idle, rising to 300–500 MB for complex SPAs with many open tabs. Startup time is 1–3 seconds; the bulk of that is loading the renderer process and initializing V8. In a pool model, keep browsers warm and measure steady-state memory rather than startup cost.

For large workloads, containerize each browser process (one container per browser) and orchestrate with Kubernetes to scale the pool horizontally. Services like Browserless and Playwright Server offer Docker images and REST/WebSocket APIs that pool browser instances across multiple clients. Puppeteer-cluster and playwright-pool are open-source Node.js libraries that implement similar pooling in-process.
Hosted headless Chrome API with pooling
Patches fingerprinting properties to reduce bot detection
Parallel WebDriver tests across many nodes

flag disables Chromium's internal process isolation. This is necessary in Docker containers running as root, but it means a compromise of the renderer process gives full access to the browser process. To compensate, run the container itself in a hardened environment: seccomp profiles, AppArmor or SELinux policies, and a read-only filesystem except for the output directory.

For reliability, set page-level timeouts and catch navigation errors explicitly. Network-unreachable pages will hang until the timeout fires rather than failing immediately. Implement circuit breakers for domains that consistently time out to avoid exhausting browser pool capacity.
Headless browsers run the complete browser engine without a visible window, making them ideal for server-side automation, CI testing, and cloud-deployed AI agents.
Chromium's unified headless mode (Chrome 112+) shares all code paths with the headed browser, eliminating the behavioral gaps that the old headless shell had.
Screenshot and PDF generation are first-class operations: call page.screenshot() or page.pdf() at any point to capture the current rendered state.
Bot-detection systems fingerprint headless browsers through navigator.webdriver and other signals; playwright-stealth patches the most common leaks.
Maintain a warm browser pool and create a new context per request rather than spawning a fresh browser process for each task.
Security in container environments requires explicit --no-sandbox plus OS-level hardening (seccomp, AppArmor) to compensate for the reduced internal sandbox.
